headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no family gets full military honors as hundreds refuse to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: Deputy AG announces new DOJ fraud division to combat 'insane' government benefit fraud
headlines.domestic: Senate moves toward passing sweeping housing bill ahead of midterm elections

metadata.daily_do: Check if your local VA cemetery needs volunteers for honor guard services - most accept civilian volunteers to ensure no veteran is buried alone

clips.caption: When Duke gets unexpectedly emotional about military brotherhood 🇺🇸
Dr. Pringle explains why Romans never left soldiers behind - and why it still matters
Jax discovers the most Florida crime story of all time

script.text: Look, I've been to too many funerals where the family shows up just to fight over the will. This guy had hundreds of strangers who just wanted to honor him.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros. It's Tuesday, March 11th, and you're listening to The Friction. Let me start with three headlines that matter today.

Geopolitics first. A Navy veteran named Lonnie Wayman died in Tennessee with no known family to claim his remains. Yesterday, hundreds of community members and fellow veterans showed up to give him full military honors at Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery.
Oh wow.
The VA representative said quote, 'unclaimed' was a misnomer given the turnout. They refused to let him be buried alone.
That's— that's actually beautiful.
Economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced the DOJ is creating an entirely new fraud division to combat what he called the 'insane' problem of government benefit fraud.
Oh here we go.

He cited cases including two Pennsylvania men who fraudulently received four million dollars for fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis. The new policy: any fraud over a thousand dollars is a felony with prison time.
Finally.
Domestic. The Senate is advancing comprehensive housing legislation ahead of midterm elections. The bill's progress is being called surprising given how contentious housing policy has become.
Wait, they're actually doing something about housing?
In an election year? Bold strategy.
We'll dig into all of that. But first, let's talk about what happened in Tennessee yesterday.
So Lonnie Wayman, Navy veteran, dies with no known relatives. His remains are sitting at the funeral home, officially labeled 'unclaimed.' But word gets out to the veteran community.
And they said absolutely not. We don't leave people behind.
Hundreds showed up. Full military honors. Honor guard, prayers, the works. They released doves at the end.
I mean, this is what community looks like, right? Like, actual community. Not the word we throw around, but people showing up for someone they never met because it's the right thing to do.
I saw the video. The church was packed, people standing outside. For a guy none of them knew.
That's the thing though— they did know him. Maybe not personally, but they knew what mattered. He served. He took an oath. That creates a bond that doesn't break just because you die alone.
But Duke, shouldn't the federal government have systems in place for this? Like, we spend seven hundred billion on defense but we can't track down next of kin for veterans?
Bree, sometimes the system isn't the point. Sometimes people just do the right thing because it's right. Not everything needs a federal program.
Actually, the VA does have protocols for unclaimed veterans. They work with state cemeteries, local veteran groups. But it's not automatic— it requires someone to make the call.
Right, so it's still dependent on community action. Which is beautiful when it works, but what about the veterans who don't get that call?
Wait, how many unclaimed veterans are we talking about? Is this a big problem?
The VA doesn't publish comprehensive numbers, but individual states report dozens per year. It's not rare.
Which is exactly why what happened yesterday matters. It sends a message. Hey, if you served, we've got you. Even at the end.
I'm not disagreeing with that sentiment, Duke. I just think we can honor veterans AND have better systems. It shouldn't be random whether your community shows up.
Can I ask a weird question? How do you even become unclaimed? Like, everybody has somebody, right?
Lot of reasons. Estranged families, no children, outlived friends. Some veterans struggle with housing, mental health— they lose touch with people.
Jenny asked me the same thing this morning. I told her, look, military service can be isolating. You serve with people for years, then you scatter. Some guys never really reconnect.
Which is why yesterday was so powerful. It's like saying, okay, the system failed you in life, but we're not failing you in death.
I keep thinking about the VA guy saying 'unclaimed' was the wrong word. Like, hundreds of people just claimed him.
Exactly. The paperwork said one thing, but the reality was completely different.
The funeral director, the honor guard, the priest— they all donated their services. Nobody charged the state.
See, that's what I mean about community. When it works, it's incredible. But it's also highlighting gaps in how we care for veterans.
Bree, sometimes the gap is the point. Sometimes people need to step up, not because they have to, but because they want to. Because it means something.
Professor, this feels like something you've seen before in history.
Indeed, Leo. The Romans had a principle: Nemo miles sine sepultura. No soldier without burial. It wasn't policy— it was sacred duty.
When a Roman legionnaire fell in battle, his brothers-in-arms would carry his body from the field. Not for the state, not for the general, but because leaving a soldier behind was unthinkable.
Even if it was dangerous?

Especially then. At the Battle of Cannae in 216 BC, after Rome's most devastating defeat, survivors spent days searching for their dead comrades in the aftermath. Hannibal's forces controlled the field, but they searched anyway.
That's exactly what happened yesterday, just two thousand years later.
Precisely. Military service creates bonds that transcend death. The Romans understood this. They built funeral pyres on foreign shores, carved names on monuments in distant provinces.
It's the same reason we have Arlington. The same reason we do military honors. Some things don't change.
What struck me about yesterday's ceremony was how spontaneous it was. No bureaucrat ordered it. No general commanded it. The community simply decided this man deserved honor.
That's very Roman. The state provided structure— the cemetery, the protocols. But the heart of it, the actual claiming of this veteran, came from ordinary citizens who understood duty.
So this has been happening for like, thousands of years?

Military burial honors appear in every civilization that values service. Greeks, Romans, medieval knights, samurai warriors. The forms change— doves instead of eagles, prayers instead of pyres— but the principle endures.
Doc, did these ancient societies have better systems for tracking their veterans than we do?

Ah, interesting question. Roman legions maintained detailed records— the acta diurna recorded births, deaths, service records. But Miss Aldana, they also had smaller populations and tighter community bonds.
What happened yesterday suggests that even in our modern, disconnected age, those bonds can still form. Strangers becoming brothers through shared respect for service.
That's it exactly. The system didn't fail— it just needed people to activate it.
Professor, thank you. Let's shift gears to some lighter news. Jax, what weird stories are floating around today?
Oh boy, do I have some good ones. First up— and I cannot believe this is real— there's a workplace harassment case in Ireland where a boss repeatedly shouted the word 'potato' at his Irish employee.
I'm sorry, what?
The employee filed a discrimination complaint saying his boss would just yell 'POTATO!' at him during meetings. Like, as harassment. Based on his ethnicity.
That's— that's actually really messed up, but also so absurd I don't know how to process it.
Did he win the case?
Still pending, but I mean, imagine being the judge hearing that testimony. 'Your honor, he said potato.' 'How many times?' 'Repeatedly, your honor. Aggressively potato'd me.'
Young man, that's actually quite serious. Ethnic harassment in the workplace—
Oh totally, Professor, you're right. It's just the word choice is so— anyway, next story. Palantir, the AI weapons company, got their contract reassigned.
Wait, reassigned to what? Please tell me they're not making scarier weapons.
Even better. They're now using their advanced artificial intelligence to help government workers find office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?
Apparently there was some procurement crisis and they needed their AI to locate available office furniture across federal agencies. From battlefield analytics to, uh, chair analytics.
This is the most government thing I've ever heard. We have AI that can track enemy movements and we're using it to find chairs.
How much are we paying for this chair-finding AI?
The contract doesn't specify, but knowing government procurement, probably enough to buy every federal employee their own throne.
You know what, at least they're not making weapons. I'll take overpriced chair-finding over killer robots.
Speaking of government efficiency, Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every classroom. That deadline's coming up fast.
Oh right, that law. Are they actually doing it?
Mixed results. Some districts are complying, others are dragging their feet, and apparently there's been a run on poster printers in Baton Rouge.
The constitutional issues aside, it's fascinating how quickly bureaucracies can mobilize when there's a hard deadline.
Right? They can't fix the school lunch program but they can print thousands of commandment posters in a week.
What's the last story, Jax?
Oh, this is the most Florida story of all time. A drug dealer in Tampa locked a customer in a storage unit for months because he couldn't pay his debt.
script.direction: building up the Florida man energy

script.text: Months?
Months. The guy survived on energy drinks and gas station snacks that the dealer would occasionally bring. Like the world's worst subscription service.
How is he still alive? How did nobody notice?
Well, it was Florida, so people probably just assumed it was a really dedicated storage unit tenant. You know, living the minimalist lifestyle.
How did he finally get out?
Storage facility employee doing routine checks heard him banging on the door. Police found him with a full beard, about thirty pounds lighter, and apparently a new appreciation for sunlight.
And the dealer?
Arrested for kidnapping, drug trafficking, and probably the worst customer service in Florida history.
I don't know whether to laugh or call my therapist.
The modern debtor's prison, I suppose. Though considerably less regulated.
Florida Man never disappoints. Though this one is more Florida Nightmare.
Alright, let's bring this back to something productive. Today's Daily Do comes from our opening story about Lonnie Wayman.
If you're moved by what happened in Tennessee, here's something you can actually do. Most VA cemeteries and state veteran cemeteries accept civilian volunteers for honor guard services.
You don't need military experience. They'll train you to fold flags, present honors, and ensure no veteran is buried alone. Google your local VA cemetery plus the words 'honor guard volunteer.'
That's actually really beautiful, Leo. Like, actionable community building.
I looked into this after my dad died. They need people, especially younger volunteers who can handle the physical parts.
Most programs ask for just a few hours a month. And according to volunteers I've talked to, it's one of the most meaningful things you can do.
Way better than finding office chairs with AI.
Professor, any final thoughts?

The Roman historian Tacitus wrote, 'The desire for glory clings even to the best men longer than any other passion.' But yesterday in Tennessee, it wasn't about glory. It was about simple human decency.
Sometimes the most important battles are fought not on foreign fields, but in our own communities, one act of respect at a time.
Thank you, Professor. That's The Friction for Tuesday, March 11th. I'm Leo Barros.
And remember, if you're ever locked in a Florida storage unit, bang louder. Someone might actually be listening.
